label: Yongbyon Nuclear Complex
sub: Primary nuclear production facility; 5MWe graphite reactor produces plutonium. Centrifuge enrichment facility revealed to Sig Hecker in 2010. IAEA confirmed reactor restart 2021. Inspectors expelled 2009.

label: Punggye-ri Nuclear Test Site
sub: Location of all six DPRK nuclear tests (2006–2017). Kim Jong-un announced closure and tunnels blown up in 2018. Satellite imagery (38 North) showed continued construction activity after 2018. Mountain partially collapsed after 2017 hydrogen bomb test.

label: Sohae Satellite Launch Station
sub: Main long-range rocket/satellite launch facility on DPRK's northwest coast. Used for all satellite launches since 2012. Kim promised to close Sohae in 2018 Singapore summit commitments; partial dismantlement observed, then reversed. Malligyong-1 military satellite launched from here November 2023.

label: Sinpo Submarine Base
sub: Primary DPRK submarine base and SLBM testing facility. Pukguksong-1 SLBM ejection tested from here in 2016. New large submarine (8,000 tonnes) unveiled 2023 likely built here. Located on East Sea (Sea of Japan) coast.

label: Tonghae / Musudan-ri Launch Site
sub: DPRK's original ballistic missile test facility, used for Nodong, Taepodong, and medium-range missile tests since the 1990s. Located on east coast near Musudan-ri (Dongchang-ri). Still active for shorter-range ballistic missile tests.

label: Pyongyang
sub: Capital of the DPRK since 1948. Population ~3 million (official). Virtually destroyed by US bombing during Korean War (1950–1953). Rebuilt as a showcase socialist capital by Kim Il-sung. Site of DPRK's major military parades, WPK congress, and diplomatic events.

label: Panmunjom / Joint Security Area
sub: Site of the 1953 Korean Armistice signing. Location of inter-Korean negotiations and US-DPRK military contacts. Scene of the 1976 Axe Murder Incident and the 2018 Moon-Kim historic handshake across the military demarcation line. Trump briefly crossed into DPRK territory here June 30, 2019.

label: Korean DMZ
sub: 248-km long, 4-km wide Demilitarized Zone dividing Korean Peninsula since 1953 armistice. Approximately 1.5 million landmines. DPRK has approximately 600,000 troops and thousands of artillery pieces positioned near the DMZ, targeting Seoul (only 55 km from border).

label: Kaesong Industrial Complex
sub: Joint South-North economic zone established 2004. At peak employed 55,000 North Korean workers in ~120 South Korean factories. Generated ~$100M/year in wages paid to DPRK. South Korea unilaterally closed complex February 2016 following fourth nuclear test. Remains shuttered.

label: Seoul
sub: Capital of South Korea; population ~10 million (25 million metro area). Only 55 km from the DMZ and within range of DPRK long-range artillery. Captured and recaptured during the Korean War. Site of 2018 Winter Olympics diplomacy when DPRK athletes marched with South Korea under unified flag.

label: Inchon Landing (Sep 15, 1950)
sub: Site of Gen. Douglas MacArthur's decisive amphibious assault behind NKPA lines, September 15, 1950. The Inchon landing cut NKPA supply lines and enabled UN forces to recapture Seoul and push north toward the Yalu River. Considered one of history's most daring military operations.

label: Battle of Chosin Reservoir (Nov–Dec 1950)
sub: Site of one of the Korean War's most brutal battles. 30,000 UN forces (mostly US Marines) encircled by 120,000 Chinese troops. 17-day fighting retreat to Hungnam; approximately 4,500 US KIA and MIA. Chinese suffered massive casualties (~25,000 dead). The 'Frozen Chosin' battle shaped Korean War outcome.

label: Hungnam Evacuation (Dec 1950)
sub: Site of the December 1950 evacuation — one of the largest in US military history. 105,000 US and ROK troops plus 91,000 North Korean civilian refugees evacuated by sea as Chinese forces advanced. Operation Chromite legacy. Industrial city of Hamhung-Hungnam largely destroyed before evacuation.

label: Wonsan — Naval Base & Launch Site
sub: Major DPRK East Sea port and naval base. USS Pueblo was intercepted near here in 1968. DPRK has launched multiple ballistic missiles from Wonsan area. Kim Jong-un uses Wonsan as a resort compound (Masikryong ski resort nearby).

label: Hamhung — Missile Research Institute
sub: Location of DPRK's primary chemical and missile research facilities, including the Hamhung Chemical Engineering Design Institute. Soviet scientists established research infrastructure here in the 1950s. Associated with DPRK's early Scud reverse-engineering program and chemical weapons production.

label: Chongjin — Famine Epicenter
sub: North Hamgyong Province capital and DPRK's third-largest city. Region among the hardest hit during the Arduous March famine (1994–1998). Adjacent to Camp 25 (Chongjin Internment Camp), a political prison facility documented by defectors. Barbara Demick's book 'Nothing to Envy' focuses on this city.

label: Hoeryong — Camp 22 (Closed ~2012)
sub: Site of Kwanliso (political prison camp) No. 22, one of the largest DPRK political prison camps, reportedly holding 30,000–50,000 prisoners at peak. Satellite imagery showed the camp was largely dismantled around 2012, possibly relocated. Defectors reported horrific conditions including chemical and biological weapons testing on prisoners.

label: Kaechon — Camp 14 (Bukchang)
sub: Kwanliso No. 14 — one of DPRK's largest and longest-running political prison camps, reportedly established in the 1950s and still active. Estimated 15,000–20,000 prisoners. Shin Dong-hyuk — the only known person born in and escaped from a DPRK total-control zone — was born and grew up here.

label: Rason Special Economic Zone
sub: DPRK's oldest special economic zone, established 1991 near the Russian and Chinese borders. China invested in Rajin port facilities; Russia connected the Rajin–Khasan railway. Limited foreign investment. UN sanctions imposed limits on coal exports via Rason — a primary revenue source. Chinese and Russian ships still active post-COVID.

label: Busan — UN Perimeter (Sep 1950)
sub: South Korea's main port city. In August–September 1950, UN and ROK forces were driven back to the 'Pusan Perimeter' — a 140-mile defensive line around Busan. Holding the perimeter was critical to preventing DPRK conquest of all Korea. MacArthur's Inchon landing broke the siege.

label: Sunan / Pyongyang — ICBM Launch Site
sub: Pyongyang's Sunan International Airport area has been used as a mobile ICBM launch site. Hwasong-17 (Nov 2022) was launched from here on a road-mobile transporter. Multiple KN-series missile tests have originated from the Pyongyang area. Road-mobile capability is key to DPRK's survivability strategy.

label: Kangson (Chollima) — Suspected Enrichment Site
sub: Suspected undeclared uranium enrichment facility identified by US intelligence and satellite analysis. First reported by The Diplomat in 2018; confirmed by 38 North satellite analysis. IAEA raised concerns about the site. DPRK has never acknowledged Kangson. If operational, it would double DPRK's enriched uranium production capacity.

label: Yeonpyeong Island — Shelling (Nov 2010)
sub: South Korean island in the Yellow Sea, near the disputed Northern Limit Line (NLL). Site of the November 23, 2010 DPRK artillery attack — the first North Korean attack on a South Korean civilian area since 1953. Two marines and two civilians killed. South Korea permanently reinforced the island's defenses.

label: Manpho — China Border Crossing
sub: Key crossing point on the Yalu River border between DPRK and China's Jilin Province. Primary route for defectors fleeing to China before reaching a third country (typically Thailand). DPRK shot and killed a Chinese citizen in a border incident in 2020. COVID border closure sealed all crossings including Manpho from January 2020.

label: Arch of Reunification (Demolished Oct 2024)
sub: Iconic 30-metre landmark on the road into Pyongyang, erected by Kim Jong-il in 2001, depicting two Korean women in traditional dress bridging North and South. Demolished October 2024 on Kim Jong-un's orders following his January 2024 declaration that South Korea was a 'hostile foreign state' — symbolizing the formal end of DPRK's reunification policy.
